titleInfo.title: Monks and Merchants. Silk Road Treasures from Northwest China, Gansu and Ningxia, 4th-7th Century

mods.note: p. 153-155: Minature stupa or scripture stupa (jingta). Northern Liang dynasty (398-439), dated by inscription to 428. Found Jiuquan District, Gansu. Dark grey limestone with incised line drawing, Chinese text, and and low-relief sculpture. height: 44.6 cm. Gansu provincial Museum, Lanzhou. so-called Gao Shanmu stupa. By Gao Bao. 153: Fourteen scripture stupas, also called sutra pillars (jingta), have been discovered so far in the Gansu corridor and in the Turfan District of Xinjiang; most are housed in the collections of the Gansu Provincial Museum and of the Dunhuang, the Jiuquan, and the Wuwei Municipal Museums. Two from the Turfan District remain in Berlin’s Museum für Indische Kunst. Of the fourteen, sevn have dates ranging from 426 to 436, and nine bear names of the lay Buddhist donorswho commissioned them. All but two were made under the Northern Liang dynasty (398-439) during the thirty-year rule of Juqu Mengxun, an ardent Buddhist who also admired traditional Chinese culture. Probably votive in function, most scripture stupas have been found at sites of former temples; this kind of scripture stupa is found only in Gansu and Turfan and is not known in north-central China. The type dissappears from Gansu after the Northen Liang but survives briefly in Turfan with the remnant of that dynasty.
